title: From Brain to Hearts: How my Thesis Found Its Rhythm
body:
When I (re)started this journey in July 2025, I was deep in neuroscience papers trying to build a theory about how a particular brain receptor might explain altered states of consciousness. It felt ambitious and distant from anything I could actually test—and I was running out of time. My visa expires at the end of September, and my degree depends on it.

Then something unexpected happened. I found a way to integrate my underground research into my thesis work in cognitive neuroscience: the ⦿-⦿. It's simple, tactile, and there's already a community of about 33 people using it informally. That opened a door.

My focus shifted from abstract brain chemistry to something I could measure: what happens to people's hearts when they move with rhythm? Specifically, I'm now asking whether spinning the ⦿-⦿ in time with music can synchronize someone's heart rate and breathing in measurable ways. It sounds niche, but it touches on something real—how our bodies entrain to external rhythms.

The design began coming into focus: I'll test whether the stick helps people sync their physiology to music, whether it matters if they're moving *with* or *against* their natural rhythm, and whether I can capture these changes using affordable heart-rate sensors.

I have a willing participant pool right here in Berlin. The hypothesis object has shifted from emotion to physiology, from theory to data. And honestly? That feels like progress.
